attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department by his family who are concerned that he "has not been his normal self over the past few days". They initially thought he was intoxicated as he is a heavy drinker and usually gets through a bottle of wine a day. However, his condition has not improved and they are worried.

On examination, he is drowsy and not oriented to time or place. He can follow two-stage commands but is not speaking coherently. His blood pressure is 110/75 mmHg, pulse 90/min, temperature 37.5oC, respiratory rate 16/min and SpO2 97% on room air. His BMI is 26. He has bilateral Dupuytren's contractures and gynaecomastia. When asked to extend his arm and cock his wrist, a flapping tremor is noted. He has no abdominal tenderness but a fluid wave is present.

Blood tests are shown below:

  • AST: 523 U/L (<45)

  • ALT: 352 U/L (<55)

  • GGT: 205 (<55)

  • Albumin: 28 g/L (35-50)

  • Platelets: 120 x109/L (150-400)

  • Bilirubin: 23 μmol/L (<20)

  • Conjugated bilirubin: 3µmol/L (<7)

  • Unconjugated bilirubin: 20µmol/L (<13)

  • CRP: 2 (< 5)

  • Aspartate aminotransferase to platelet ratio index (APRI): 4.5 (<1)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient has presented with a clinical picture of acute confusion (not orientated to location and time) and asterixis that is most likely attributed to hepatic encephalopathy, from alcoholic cirrhosis. His reduced hepatic synthetic function and deranged liver function tests (AST/ALT >1) and aspartate aminotransferase to platelet ratio index also support decompensated alcoholic cirrhosis as the underlying cause of his confusion.

The first-line treatment for acute hepatic encephalopathy is lactulose orally every one-to-two hours until a laxative effect has been achieved. Lactulose has a rapid laxative action and works by decreasing the intestinal production and absorption of ammonia and other nitrogenous wastes that contribute to hepatic encephalopathy. It is also used prophylactically to prevent recurrent hepatic encephalopathy, or to treat patients with chronic hepatic encephalopathy.

Rifaximin is an antibiotic that is not absorbed into the systemic circulation and acts locally in the gastrointestinal system. It is bactericidal and targets a wide range of bacteria, which include bacteria that degrade nitrogenous wastes. This antibiotic is used as hepatic encephalopathy prophylaxis in patients not responsive to prophylactic lactulose monotherapy. It is not used in the treatment of acute hepatic encephalopathy.

Docusate senna is a medication that is commonly used to treat constipation and has no role in the management of hepatic encephalopathy.

Ceftriaxone is a broad-spectrum third generation cephalosporin. It is used in spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Patients with cirrhosis and ascites are at risk of developing spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, which can then lead to delirium. This patient has a fluid wave, suggesting ascites is present. However, the absence of abdominal tenderness, fever and a normal CRP make spontaneous bacterial peritonitis causing hepatic encephalopathy less likely.

Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine that is used in the context of alcohol withdrawal. It is a GABA agonist which reduces the symptoms of alcohol withdrawal. However, the absence of tachycardia and postural tremor make alcohol withdrawal less likely.
learningPoints: In patients with hepatic encephalopathy, lactulose should be prescribed every two hours to induce a rapid laxative effect
references: Leise MD, Poterucha JJ, Kamath PS, Kim WR. Management of hepatic encephalopathy in the hospital. Mayo Clin Proc. 2014 Feb;89(2):241-53. doi: 10.1016/j.mayocp.2013.11.009. Epub 2014 Jan 8. PMID: 24411831; PMCID: PMC4128786.
Gramlich L, Tandon P & Rahman A. (2021) Nutritional status in patients with sustained heavy alcohol use. In T.W. Post, P. Rutgeerts, & S. Grover (Eds.), UptoDate.
Hepatic encephalopathy. Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited. Available from: https://www.tg.org.au
